summary: The backend container follows a classic layered architecture using Spring Boot, exposing REST controllers that directly call service implementations which in turn access JPA repositories. It models a rich legal‑document lifecycle domain with over 300 entity classes and dozens of services, but suffers from moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion, leading to a grade of C. Key pain points include oversized services, leakage of DAO implementations into the service layer, lack of explicit interfaces, and missing DTO boundaries. Refactoring toward clearer aggregate roots, a dedicated use‑case layer, and stricter interface‑driven design will markedly improve maintainability and scalability.

capabilities: Create, read, update, delete (CRUD) deed entries and related registry data
Handle handover data sets and successor batches
Perform cryptographic key management and re‑encryption
Archive and re‑archive documents with signed tokens
Process actions and background jobs (scheduling, workers)
Log deed entry activity and generate reports
Expose comprehensive REST APIs for external consumption
functional.bounded_context: Legal Document Lifecycle Management (Deed Management, Archiving and Cryptographic Key Handling)
integrations: Relational Database via Spring Data JPA / Hibernate

debt_indicators: Very large number of Entity classes (360) suggests over‑modeling or lack of aggregation
Service layer contains DAO implementation classes (e.g., DeedEntryConnectionDaoImpl) indicating layered leakage
Controllers directly expose REST interfaces without a façade or use‑case layer
Potential God services – 42 service implementations, many likely handling multiple responsibilities
Missing explicit domain service interfaces; services are concrete classes only
High count of REST interface definitions (21) duplicated across controllers and services
recommendations: Introduce a use‑case / application service layer between Controllers and Domain Services to decouple REST handling from business logic
Group related entities into aggregate roots to reduce entity count and improve domain cohesion
Extract interfaces for all services and inject them; avoid concrete class references in Controllers
Refactor oversized services into smaller, single‑responsibility services (e.g., split ArchiveManagerServiceImpl, KeyManagerServiceImpl)
Apply proper repository abstraction: keep DAO implementations out of the Service layer and inject repositories via interfaces
Implement DTOs and mappers to separate persistence models (entities) from API models, reducing coupling between layers
Add package‑by‑feature modularization to enforce bounded context boundaries and prevent cross‑context leakage

overall_assessment: The backend provides comprehensive functionality for legal document lifecycle management but is hampered by architectural drift and technical debt. Moderate coupling and cohesion, together with a high volume of entities and monolithic services, limit scalability and maintainability. Addressing these issues with a clearer separation of concerns and domain‑driven design will substantially raise the quality grade.
top_recommendations: Add a dedicated use‑case/application service layer to isolate REST controllers from business logic.
Refactor large services into focused, single‑responsibility services and introduce interfaces for dependency injection.
Consolidate entities into aggregate roots and employ DTOs/mappers to decouple persistence models from API contracts.
